stem: A differentiable function \( f \) satisfies \( f'(x) = ([[a]])x^{2} + ([[b]]) \) and \( f([[x0]]) = [[y0]] \). Find \( f([[x1]]) \).
solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, \( f([[x1]]) = f([[x0]]) + \int_{[[x0]]}^{[[x1]]} f'(t)\, dt = ([[y0]]) + \int_{[[x0]]}^{[[x1]]} \left( ([[a]])t^{2} + ([[b]]) \right) dt = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the net change \( \int_{[[x0]]}^{[[x1]]} f' \) but forgets to add the initial value \( f([[x0]]) = [[y0]] \).
This adds \( f'([[x1]]) \) to the initial value instead of integrating the derivative.
This integrates from 0 rather than from the given point \( x = [[x0]] \).
This subtracts the accumulated change instead of adding it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A differentiable function \( f \) satisfies \( f'(x) = e^{[[n]] x} \) and \( f(\ln [[p]]) = [[y0]] \). Find \( f(\ln [[q]]) \).
solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, \( f(\ln [[q]]) = f(\ln [[p]]) + \int_{\ln [[p]]}^{\ln [[q]]} e^{[[n]]t}\, dt \). Since \( \int e^{[[n]]t}\,dt = \tfrac{1}{[[n]]}e^{[[n]]t} \) and \( e^{[[n]]\ln[[q]]}=[[q]]^{[[n]]} \), this is \( ([[y0]]) + \frac{[[q]]^{[[n]]} - [[p]]^{[[n]]}}{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the net change alone but forgets to add the initial value \( f(\ln [[p]]) = [[y0]] \).
This forgets to divide by \( [[n]] \) from the antiderivative \( \tfrac{1}{[[n]]}e^{[[n]]t} \).
This integrates from \( t=0 \) instead of from the given point \( t=\ln[[p]] \).
This subtracts the accumulated change instead of adding it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A twice-differentiable function \( f \) satisfies \( f''(x) = [[a]] \) (constant), \( f'([[x0]]) = [[v0]] \), and \( f([[x0]]) = [[y0]] \). Find \( f([[x1]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the first integration (a linear model), dropping the quadratic contribution of \( f'' \) entirely.
This uses only the SECOND integration's quadratic term, dropping the contribution of the initial velocity \( f'([[x0]]) \).
This forgets to add the initial value \( f([[x0]]) = [[y0]] \) after integrating twice.
This forgets to divide by 2 when integrating the constant second derivative a second time.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A particle moves along a line with velocity \( v(t) = ([[a]])t + ([[b]]) \) meters per second. At time \( t=[[t0]] \) seconds, its position is \( s([[t0]]) = [[s0]] \) meters. Find its position \( s([[t1]]) \) at time \( t=[[t1]] \) seconds.
solution: Position is the antiderivative of velocity. By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, \( s([[t1]]) = s([[t0]]) + \int_{[[t0]]}^{[[t1]]} v(t)\, dt = ([[s0]]) + \int_{[[t0]]}^{[[t1]]} \left(([[a]])t+([[b]])\right) dt = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the net displacement alone but forgets to add the initial position \( s([[t0]]) = [[s0]] \).
This adds \( v([[t1]]) \) to the initial position instead of integrating the velocity over the elapsed time.
This integrates from \( t=0 \) instead of from the given time \( t=[[t0]] \).
This subtracts the net displacement instead of adding it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A differentiable function \( f \) satisfies \( f'(x) = [[a]] \) (constant) and \( f([[p]]) = [[yp]] \). Find the value of \( x \) for which \( f(x) = [[y0]] \).
solution: Since \( f'(x)=([[a]]) \) is constant, \( f(x) = ([[yp]]) + ([[a]])(x-([[p]])) \). Setting this equal to \( [[y0]] \) and solving for \( x \) gives \( x = [[p]] + \dfrac{[[y0]]-([[yp]])}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This otherwise solves correctly but is shifted by an extra 1.
This flips the sign of the shift term when isolating \( x \).
This drops the reference point \( p \) from the answer entirely.
This multiplies by \( p \) instead of adding it as the reference point.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
